summary: Graham argues that iPhones, iPads, and similar devices are best understood as tablets, and that their real significance is enabling a wave of “ephemeralization” where physical objects are replaced by software. This shift will make many specialized devices unnecessary, creating big opportunities for startups and platform competition beyond Apple.
key_points: The term “tablet” better captures the new class of devices than “mobile devices.”
Tablets enable software to replace many specialized physical objects.
This creates near-term startup opportunities by spotting things tablets can make unnecessary.
Ephemeralization is a powerful historical force driving tablet adoption.
Platform openness and developer access will determine competition with Apple.
money_quotes: The iPhone isn't so much a phone as a replacement for a phone.
Many if not most of the special-purpose objects around us are going to be replaced by apps running on tablets.
The advantages of doing things in software on a single device are so great that everything that can get turned into software will.
The iPhone and the iPad have effectively drilled a hole that will allow ephemeralization to flow into a lot of new areas.
Give hackers an inch and they'll take you a mile.
key_insight: Tablets matter less as devices than as a platform for software to replace physical objects, unlocking a broad wave of opportunity and competition.
